title: The Collapse of Federal Funding for Non-profits
gist: Daniel Stid argues the 60-year hybrid system of federally funded, non-profit-delivered social services has collapsed under the second Trump administration, but that the collapse was the culmination of decades of structural contradictions that co-opted non-profits into the state; he contends restoration is both impossible and undesirable, and that a new system should shift toward state/local and philanthropic funding of direct services.

insights.claim: The hybrid system of federally funded, non-profit-provided services that grew since the mid-1960s has collapsed, with the second Trump administration enacting grant cancellations, freezes, impoundments and rescissions that have already cost tens of thousands of non-profit jobs.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: A structural revenue source for the entire non-profit social-services sector is being withdrawn, forcing sector-wide business-model change.
insights.quote: this hybrid system of federally funded, non-profit-provided services has collapsed

insights.claim: The One Big Beautiful Bill's spending cuts will further reduce federal funding for non-profits even as they increase demand for non-profit services.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: A widening funding-demand gap is coming for social-service providers, a stress signal for the sector and the populations it serves.
insights.quote: The spending cuts enacted in the One Big Beautiful Bill will further crimp federal funding for non-profits, even while increasing demand for their services.

insights.claim: Stid argues the collapse cannot be attributed solely to Trump but resulted from decades of mounting internal contradictions that eroded the system's foundations and legitimacy.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: Frames the disruption as structural, not cyclical, so a future administration is unlikely to simply restore it.
insights.quote: Its sudden toppling resulted from mounting contradictions that ate away at its foundations and legitimacy for decades, like so many termites.

themes.theme: Origins: mediating structures and conservative authorship

insights.claim: Stid notes that 50 years ago conservatives, not the left, championed the emerging hybrid system, citing the 1977 AEI pamphlet 'To Empower People' by Peter Berger and Richard John Neuhaus.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: Reframes the current right-wing attack on non-profit funding as repudiating a system the right originally built.
insights.quote: 50 years ago, conservatives stood in the vanguard of those championing the hybrid system then emerging
insights.locator: THE RISE OF THE HYBRID SYSTEM

insights.claim: Berger and Neuhaus's 'mediating structures' framework held that neighborhoods, families, religious congregations and voluntary groups could be enlisted and funded by government to carry out public policy without the alienation of direct bureaucracy.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: Identifies the intellectual blueprint that justified decades of public-private service delivery.
insights.quote: wherever possible, public policy should utilize mediating structures for the realization of social purposes
insights.locator: THE RISE OF THE HYBRID SYSTEM

insights.claim: Senator Daniel Patrick Moynihan warned the non-profit sector in a 1980 Independent Sector address that government funding would squeeze it out of existence or subvert it into a vassal of its government paymasters.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: Establishes that the dependency risk now realized was foreseen four decades ago.
insights.quote: Little by little, you are being squeezed out of existence or slowly absorbed

insights.claim: Smith and Lipsky's 1993 'Nonprofits for Hire' found the contracting regime forced non-profit managers to hustle for contracts and bend priorities to government counterparts, masking real growth in government's scope.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: Shows the dependency critique was empirically documented and bipartisan in origin.
insights.quote: more dependence on nonprofit organizations means not less but more government involvement in the affairs of voluntary and community agencies

insights.claim: Brian O'Connell argued in 1996 that long-term government support turns a non-profit into a quasi-governmental entity with decreasing value as an independent force, even while distracting it from advocacy and citizen empowerment.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: Names the legitimacy cost of dependency that the current collapse exposes.
insights.quote: long-term government support of an agency or complex of agencies creates a quasi-governmental entity with decreasing value as an independent force

insights.claim: Bush's compassionate-conservatism agenda to put faith-based non-profits on equal footing for federal grants collapsed amid post-2000-election partisanship and the post-9/11 shift to counterterrorism.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
entities: Office of Faith-Based and Community Initiatives
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that reform requires sustained bipartisanship that the system never sustained.
insights.quote: The window for conservative reform, open all too briefly, had closed.
insights.locator: THE RISE AND FALL OF COMPASSIONATE CONSERVATISM

insights.claim: Obama's social-innovation agenda of 'find what works, fund what works' and pay-for-success/social-impact bonds operated only at the margins of the budget and was discontinued by the first Trump administration.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: Shows evidence-based reform never reached the scale of the dominant funding flows it sought to change.
insights.quote: these innovations came at the margins of the federal budget

insights.claim: Peter Rossi's 'iron law of evaluation' and the finding that nine of ten gold-standard evaluations since 1990 showed weak or no positive effects undercut the case that reformed programs could deliver at national scale.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: Provides the empirical pessimism that helped delegitimize the whole spend-on-non-profits model.
insights.quote: Nine of these evaluations found weak or no positive effects.

insights.claim: Stid argues incumbent non-profits formed a 'social services industrial complex' whose scale, contracting savvy and political influence made existing funding flows nearly impossible to redirect.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: Explains why reform failed and why disruptive actors, not reformers, ultimately broke the system.

themes.theme: Leviathan by proxy and democratic harm

insights.claim: John DiIulio argues the real 'deep state' is the contractor state, with non-profits including hospitals and universities receiving about 30 percent of their over $2 trillion in annual revenue from government grants and fees.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: Quantifies how deeply the non-profit sector's revenue base depends on the government it is now losing.
insights.quote: nonprofits registered with the I.R.S. receive about 30 percent of their more than $2 trillion a year in revenues from government grants plus fees

insights.claim: DiIulio's 'Leviathan by proxy' thesis holds that federal spending rose fivefold since 1960 while the civilian workforce stayed near 2 million, because officials outsourced administration to contractors and non-profits, creating diffuse and unaccountable bureaucracy.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: Reframes apparent small-government as hidden big-government, a critique fueling the current populist backlash.

insights.claim: Claire Dunning's 'Nonprofit Neighborhoods' shows that in Boston the most economically distressed neighborhoods have the most neighborhood non-profits, with inequality and non-profit governance growing together rather than apart.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: Bottom-up evidence that government-funded non-profits can entrench rather than reduce inequality, eroding the system's moral case.
insights.quote: the city's most economically and socially distressed neighborhoods in the twenty-first century are also those with the most neighborhood nonprofits

insights.claim: Stid argues restoration is a non-starter while the MAGA faction dominates the GOP and given mounting interest payments and Social Security/Medicare shortfalls, and that restoration is undesirable anyway.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: Signals to funders and non-profits that planning should assume permanent loss of the old federal flow, not a temporary disruption.
insights.quote: the consensus needed at the federal level to reconstruct the hybrid system is a non-starter

insights.claim: Stid recommends non-profits shift toward state and local government funding and pursue holistic, neighborhood-based solutions rather than federal individual-targeted programs.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: Points to where social-service demand and funding will migrate, relevant for sector and municipal-budget exposure.
insights.quote: non-profits should move away from depending on the now unreliable federal government and work more with state and local governments

insights.claim: Stid argues the philanthropic pendulum is swinging back from 'systems change' strategies toward funding effective direct services where the federal government is retrenching.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: Signals a reallocation of foundation dollars from advocacy toward direct service delivery.
insights.quote: The pendulum is now swinging back.

insights.claim: Stid proposes new legal-entity formats, analogous to charter schools, for non-profits primarily funded by government that are neither agencies, for-profit contractors, nor privately funded charities.
insights.speaker: Daniel Stid
insights.so_what: A structural-reform idea worth watching for how government-dependent service providers may be reclassified.
insights.quote: we may also need new legal-entity formats that better reflect how non-profits primarily funded by the government operate
